items.source: Avoma call 2026-06-22 (Ming Li) - referenced as context only; no named vendor confirmed, so not entered as a competitor entity

metrics.narrative: Ming Li quantified two concrete pain metrics on the 4 Jun 2026 call: on-time payment currently ~75% with a target of ~95% ('if we are able... improve from 75% to 95%... it would be very convincing to our CPO'), and supplier onboarding currently 20-30 days across 6-7 approval gates with a target of ~1 week. These are real, named metrics, but they remain Ming's internal talking points for building a business case rather than an EB-owned, quantified ROI model.

champion.narrative: Ming Li is a genuine, active champion: he pitched an AI adoption strategy to the CPO, created and runs an internal 'AI investor group' chat to broadcast Zycus updates, and personally expanded the 17 Jun 2026 demo beyond the North American invite list to include a 6-7 person European Operational Excellence Team. However, he explicitly self-describes as siloed - 'I'm not involve all other technology stuff... they are the backend people' (22 Jun 2026) - meaning he has no budget authority and limited influence over Josh's digital-tools team, which actually controls the vendor decision. Developing, not yet a fully enabled champion.

competition.narrative: SAP Ariba is the named incumbent (SF Competitors__c field) and is being actively piloted in parallel by the buyer's own operational-excellence team for RFQ/sourcing automation ('they are trying to see what Ariba can come out with first' - 4 Jun 2026). As of the 22 Jun 2026 call Ming confirmed 'haven't seen any user case success yet' from that Ariba exploration, meaning the incumbent has not yet delivered but remains the default first option the buyer tests before considering a new vendor.

identify_pain.narrative: Pain is well articulated and specific: scattered email-based invoice/request processing, a disjointed Ariba-plus-separate-CLM setup, manual RFI/RFX/bid evaluation, ~75% on-time payment performance, and 20-30 day supplier onboarding across 6-7 approval gates (IT, finance, cybersecurity, supplier quality). Confirmed directly by Ming Li across the 12 May and 4 Jun 2026 calls.

paper_process.narrative: No commercial, pricing, or contracting discussion has occurred in any of the four read touchpoints (12 May, 4 Jun, 17 Jun, 22 Jun 2026). Ming explicitly stated on 22 Jun 2026 that no budget beyond the ERP integration has been approved for 2026, so paper process has not started.

economic_buyer.narrative: The economic buyer is the CPO (unnamed, Paris-based), per Ming Li on the 22 Jun 2026 call. Ming presented an AI adoption strategy recommendation to the CPO around 8 Jun 2026 (two weeks prior) but as of 22 Jun 2026 has received no response, no budget approval, and no mandate. Zycus has never had direct contact with the CPO; all EB context is secondhand through Ming.

decision_process.narrative: Ming described a multi-layer process: he authored an AI adoption strategy that must be approved by the CPO; the digital-tools/operational-excellence team (led by Josh) controls tool budget and vendor selection; and the entire process is sequenced behind a 1-2 year Suez-Veolia ERP consolidation decision ('we had to make that decision first, and then after that, they will push the enhancement of these AI tools' - 22 Jun 2026). No formal timeline or gate criteria have been confirmed.

decision_criteria.narrative: The buyer has articulated informal criteria: a live demo showing Zycus working alongside SAP/Ariba without requiring ERP replacement, and proof against a specific pain point (on-time payment rate) rather than a generic walkthrough. Ming stated on 4 Jun 2026, 'do you think you can demonstrate how you work with a customer who is using SAP and how your system integrates with their system.' No formal weighted criteria or RFP exists.

north_star_verdict.math: Created 20 May 2026, 49 days in Qualified stage with no progression signals. Close date 16 Oct 2026 (100 days out) requires moving through Formal Evaluation, Shortlisted, Vendor Selected, Negotiation, and Contract stages in 14 weeks—impossible given the stated 1-2 year integration gate and frozen budget. No Qualified or Formal Eval submission dates on record. Last activity 22 Jun (16 days ago) was a discovery call where the buyer confirmed the budget freeze and integration priority. Zero forward slip recorded because the deal has never advanced past initial discovery. Required pace to hit Oct-26: immediate EB engagement, budget approval, RFI/RFP launch, and vendor selection within 60 days—none of which can occur under the current freeze. Realistic timeline per buyer: 2027-2028 post-integration, making current close date off by 12-24 months.
evidence: No approved 2026 budget. Ming confirmed on 22 Jun call: 'so far this year, we have no budget for any of these type of tools other than the integration'. The operational-excellence/digital-tools team has frozen discretionary spend until Suez-Veolia integration completes (1-2 year timeline).

Economic Buyer unengaged. The CPO (Paris-based) received Ming's AI adoption strategy presentation around 8 Jun 2026 but gave no response, no budget approval, no mandate as of 22 Jun (30+ days silent). Ming has no EB access and cannot drive a commercial decision.

Suez-Veolia integration is the stated gate. Ming: 'they didn't approve any advancement budget... only for the integration' and 'we had to wait until net happen, and then after net, we might be able to try to push your AI stuff into the agenda'. Integration timeline is 1-2 years, blocking any new tool approval including Zycus.

Incumbent Ariba exploration running in parallel. Operational-excellence team (Josh) is testing Ariba's AI/RFQ automation as the default path before approving a new vendor. Ming: 'they are trying to see what Ariba can come out with first'. If Ariba delivers a passable pilot during the freeze, inertia may block Zycus adoption.

Champion Ming is organizationally siloed. He is North America strategic sourcing lead with no budget authority, no EB access, and limited influence over the operational-excellence/digital-tools team (Josh) that controls vendor decisions. Ming: 'I'm not involve other all other technology stuff... they are the backend people'. Single-threaded on a champion with strong operational reach but weak executive/commercial influence.

No pricing or commercial discussion has occurred. The $100k amount appears to be a placeholder; Veolia has ~1,000 global procurement staff and a comprehensive S2P deployment could be multi-million/multi-year, but no scoping conversation has taken place because the deal has not progressed past demo/discovery.

Last activity 22 Jun was a discovery call (16 days ago, still recent), but it surfaced the freeze, not progression. No demo has been delivered, no RFI/RFP launched, no multi-threading beyond Ming and a few operational stakeholders (Josh, Fernando, Omar) who cannot approve budget.
north_star_verdict.headline: Deal is structurally blocked by a 1-2 year ERP/tools integration freeze with no approved budget, no Economic Buyer engagement, and no credible path to the Oct-26 close date. Champion Ming is organizationally siloed with no commercial authority. The buyer explicitly stated no 2026 advancement budget exists beyond Suez integration spend, and the CPO has not responded to Ming's AI pitch after 30 days. Veolia is evaluating incumbent Ariba's AI capabilities as the default path before considering new vendors. Without executive sponsorship, a parallel pilot carve-out, or a realistic 2027-2028 timeline, this deal will remain dormant indefinitely.

flags: Champion multi-thread: Ming Li is a strong, active champion (AI lead, sourcing director) but has NO budget authority or EB access. Josh (North America procurement digital lead, tool/budget owner) is the critical co-champion to cultivate—he controls the operational-excellence team's vendor decisions and has direct line to the CPO/exec team. Fernando (P2P director) and Omar (regional buyer manager) are influential P2P pain owners who can advocate for a pilot. Build a coalition: arm all four with a concise, CPO-ready pilot business case (quantified P2P impact, ERP-safe architecture, 90-day proof-of-value) so when the budget gate opens they can jointly sponsor it. Single-thread risk is HIGH if Ming alone carries this through a 1-2 year freeze.

Pilot-first wedge against the integration freeze: The buyer's stated objection is 'we need to complete the Suez integration and decide the ERP platform first, THEN consider new tools' (1-2 year timeline). Challenge that sequencing with a pilot-first approach: a narrow, high-impact P2P pilot (on-time payment 75%→95%, supplier onboarding 20-30d→1wk) can run IN PARALLEL to the integration because Zycus is ERP-complementary (no SAP replacement required) and modular (deploy P2P independently, expand post-integration). Frame it as 'validate the AI ROI now on a contained use case so you can confidently scale it across the consolidated organization later, rather than wait 2 years and start from zero'. This is the ONLY path to pull the close date forward; otherwise, requalify to 2027-2028 pipeline. Position the pilot as a strategic hedge—if Ariba's automation pilots fail (as they have so far), Veolia needs a proven alternative ready to deploy when the integration completes.

Competitive displacement strategy vs Ariba: Ariba is the incumbent with low adoption and no successful AI pilots yet, but they have the operational-excellence team's attention by default ('let's see what Ariba can do first'). Our counter: build a direct comparison narrative—'Ariba is a point-solution bolt-on with limited AI depth; Zycus is the comprehensive, AI-native source-to-pay platform with proven P2P automation (share 2-3 customer proof points: on-time payment improvement, supplier onboarding acceleration, tail-spend savings via ANA). Ariba will incrementally patch; Zycus leapfrogs.' Share this narrative WITH Ming/Josh as a Leave-Behind they can use internally, and offer a bake-off pilot: 'Run a 90-day Zycus P2P pilot alongside Ariba's RFQ automation test; measure which delivers measurable operational improvement.' This forces a data-driven decision and neutralizes the incumbent advantage. If Ariba's pilots continue to underwhelm, we become the obvious choice when budget reopens.

SAP Ariba is being tested internally as the default RFQ/sourcing path with 'no user case success yet' as of 22 Jun 2026; reinforce Zycus's agentic AI differentiation now, before Ariba shows any progress.

Convert Ming Li's one quantifiable ask (on-time payment 75% -> 95%) into a scoped mini business case he can hand the CPO directly, instead of pitching the full suite; he explicitly said one proven win would be 'very convincing to our CPO' (4 Jun 2026).

Track the SAP Ariba parallel pilot (no user-case success as of 22 Jun 2026); keep fresh proof points ready so a 'good enough incumbent' result doesn't close the door before Zycus is formally evaluated.

Recorded close date (16 Oct 2026) is not credible against the buyer's own 1-2 year ERP-consolidation statement; internally re-baseline the expected close while keeping the relationship warm via the Horizon conference invite and regular updates.
best_practice_check.summary: The deal needs a second thread with actual budget influence, a scaled commercial ask that can survive the ERP-consolidation freeze, and a realistic close date internally.

customer_preference.evidence: Buyer-side P2P leaders reacted positively to the 17 Jun 2026 demo ('they were also impressed... we should do better,' per Ming's 22 Jun 2026 recap), but no buyer has voiced an explicit preference for Zycus over SAP Ariba.

competitive_position.summary: SAP Ariba is the sole confirmed competitor: the incumbent ERP-adjacent procurement tool, currently being piloted by the buyer's own operational-excellence team for sourcing/RFQ automation in parallel with the Zycus conversation. As of 22 Jun 2026 that pilot has not produced a successful use case, leaving the field genuinely open, but Ariba remains the default 'try first' path given the existing SAP relationship.

No Economic Buyer engaged after 37 days and a well-received demo. CPO (Paris) received Ming's AI adoption strategy but gave no response, no budget approval, no mandate. The operational-excellence team (Josh) and P2P directors (Fernando, Omar) are influencers, not budget holders. Without EB access and executive sponsorship, this deal cannot progress to proposal or negotiation. Recommend: brief the deal owner's manager on the EB gap and request an executive connect (Zycus exec to the relevant stakeholder or CFO) to validate the business case, discuss the integration-freeze challenge, and explore pilot budget exception.

Parallel pilot path required to overcome the 1-2 year integration freeze. Buyer has mandated that all new tool approvals wait until the Veolia-Suez integration completes (2027-2028) and has approved zero 2026 budget for Zycus. Waiting until 2027-2028 risks losing the deal to Ariba inertia (good-enough incumbent pilot) or a new ERP vendor's bundled modules. Recommend: position a limited-scope P2P proof-of-concept (e.g. pay-on-time improvement 75% → 95% in one North America region) that runs DURING the integration, requires minimal IT lift, delivers quantifiable results in 3-6 months, and does not gate or delay the integration. Frame it as an AI adoption learning initiative (aligned with the relevant stakeholder recommendation) that de-risks the full platform decision in 2027-2028. Engage Josh to champion this pilot path internally.

Josh (North America procurement digital lead, tool/budget owner) is the critical co-champion to cultivate—he controls the operational-excellence team's vendor decisions and has direct line to the CPO/exec team. Fernando (P2P director) and Omar (regional buyer manager) are influential P2P pain owners who can advocate for a pilot. Build a coalition: arm all four with a concise, CPO-ready pilot business case (quantified P2P impact, ERP-safe architecture, 90-day proof-of-value) so when the budget gate opens they can jointly sponsor it. Pilot-first wedge against the integration freeze: The buyer's stated objection is 'we need to complete the Suez integration and decide the ERP platform first, THEN consider new tools' (1-2 year timeline). Challenge that sequencing with a pilot-first approach: a narrow, high-impact P2P pilot (on-time payment 75%→95%, supplier onboarding 20-30d→1wk) can run IN PARALLEL to the integration because Zycus is ERP-complementary (no SAP replacement required) and modular (deploy P2P independently, expand post-integration). Position the pilot as a strategic hedge—if Ariba's automation pilots fail (as they have so far), Veolia needs a proven alternative ready to deploy when the integration completes.

Competitive displacement strategy vs Ariba: Ariba is the incumbent with low adoption and no successful AI pilots yet, but they have the operational-excellence team's attention by default ('let's see what Ariba can do first'). Our counter: build a direct comparison narrative—'Ariba is a point-solution bolt-on with limited AI depth; Zycus is the comprehensive, AI-native source-to-pay platform with proven P2P automation (share 2-3 customer proof points: on-time payment improvement, supplier onboarding acceleration, tail-spend savings via ANA). This forces a data-driven decision and neutralizes the incumbent advantage. If Ariba's pilots continue to underwhelm, we become the obvious choice when budget reopens.

deltas.subject: Champion multi-thread: Ming Li is a strong, active champion (AI lead, sourcing director) but has NO budget authority or EB access. Josh (North America procurement digital lead, tool/budget owner) is the critical co-champion to cultivate—he controls the operational-excellence team's vendor decisions and has direct line to the CPO/exec team. Fernando (P2P director) and Omar (regional buyer manager) are influential P2P pain owners who can advocate for a pilot. Build a coalition: arm all four with a concise, CPO-ready pilot business case (quantified P2P impact, ERP-safe architecture, 90-day proof-of-value) so when the budget gate opens they can jointly sponsor it. Single-thread risk is HIGH if Ming alone carries this through a 1-2 year freeze.

deltas.to: Pilot-first wedge against the integration freeze: The buyer's stated objection is 'we need to complete the Suez integration and decide the ERP platform first, T
deltas.subject: Pilot-first wedge against the integration freeze: The buyer's stated objection is 'we need to complete the Suez integration and decide the ERP platform first, THEN consider new tools' (1-2 year timeline). Challenge that sequencing with a pilot-first approach: a narrow, high-impact P2P pilot (on-time payment 75%→95%, supplier onboarding 20-30d→1wk) can run IN PARALLEL to the integration because Zycus is ERP-complementary (no SAP replacement required) and modular (deploy P2P independently, expand post-integration). Frame it as 'validate the AI ROI now on a contained use case so you can confidently scale it across the consolidated organization later, rather than wait 2 years and start from zero'. This is the ONLY path to pull the close date forward; otherwise, requalify to 2027-2028 pipeline. Position the pilot as a strategic hedge—if Ariba's automation pilots fail (as they have so far), Veolia needs a proven alternative ready to deploy when the integration completes.

deltas.to: Competitive displacement strategy vs Ariba: Ariba is the incumbent with low adoption and no successful AI pilots yet, but they have the operational-excellence t
deltas.subject: Competitive displacement strategy vs Ariba: Ariba is the incumbent with low adoption and no successful AI pilots yet, but they have the operational-excellence team's attention by default ('let's see what Ariba can do first'). Our counter: build a direct comparison narrative—'Ariba is a point-solution bolt-on with limited AI depth; Zycus is the comprehensive, AI-native source-to-pay platform with proven P2P automation (share 2-3 customer proof points: on-time payment improvement, supplier onboarding acceleration, tail-spend savings via ANA). Ariba will incrementally patch; Zycus leapfrogs.' Share this narrative WITH Ming/Josh as a Leave-Behind they can use internally, and offer a bake-off pilot: 'Run a 90-day Zycus P2P pilot alongside Ariba's RFQ automation test; measure which delivers measurable operational improvement.' This forces a data-driven decision and neutralizes the incumbent advantage. If Ariba's pilots continue to underwhelm, we become the obvious choice when budget reopens.

value.flag: Champion multi-thread: Ming Li is a strong, active champion (AI lead, sourcing director) but has NO budget authority or EB access. Josh (North America procurement digital lead, tool/budget owner) is the critical co-champion to cultivate—he controls the operational-excellence team's vendor decisions and has direct line to the CPO/exec team. value.flag: Pilot-first wedge against the integration freeze: The buyer's stated objection is 'we need to complete the Suez integration and decide the ERP platform first, THEN consider new tools' (1-2 year timeline). Challenge that sequencing with a pilot-first approach: a narrow, high-impact P2P pilot (on-time payment 75%→95%, supplier onboarding 20-30d→1wk) can run IN PARALLEL to the integration because Zycus is ERP-complementary (no SAP replacement required) and modular (deploy P2P independently, expand post-integration). Frame it as 'validate the AI ROI now on a contained use case so you can confidently scale it across the consolidated organization later, rather than wait 2 years and start from zero'. This is the ONLY path to pull the close date forward; otherwise, requalify to 2027-2028 pipeline. Position the pilot as a strategic hedge—if Ariba's automation pilots fail (as they have so far), Veolia needs a proven alternative ready to deploy when the integration completes.

value.flag: Competitive displacement strategy vs Ariba: Ariba is the incumbent with low adoption and no successful AI pilots yet, but they have the operational-excellence team's attention by default ('let's see what Ariba can do first'). Our counter: build a direct comparison narrative—'Ariba is a point-solution bolt-on with limited AI depth; Zycus is the comprehensive, AI-native source-to-pay platform with proven P2P automation (share 2-3 customer proof points: on-time payment improvement, supplier onboarding acceleration, tail-spend savings via ANA). Ariba will incrementally patch; Zycus leapfrogs.' Share this narrative WITH Ming/Josh as a Leave-Behind they can use internally, and offer a bake-off pilot: 'Run a 90-day Zycus P2P pilot alongside Ariba's RFQ automation test; measure which delivers measurable operational improvement.' This forces a data-driven decision and neutralizes the incumbent advantage. If Ariba's pilots continue to underwhelm, we become the obvious choice when budget reopens.

Frame it as an AI adoption learning initiative (aligned with the relevant stakeholder recommendation) that de-risks the full platform decision in 2027-2028. Engage Josh to champion this pilot path internally.
